title: Post-Acquisition Indemnity Failures: What VPS Healthcare’s Rs15.86 Crore Legal Battle Teaches Dealmakers
description:
VPS Healthcare's acquisition of Rockland Hospitals offers a stark cautionary tale in M&A structuring, revealing significant structural weaknesses in its post-acquisition indemnity framework. A disclosed ₹10 crore EY arbitration claim escalated into a seven-year legal battle, culminating in a Supreme Court ruling that dissects the drafting and structuring failures inherent in the deal.

The transaction, involving an SPA and a subsequent SIAC Consent Award, aimed to manage the known EY liability. However, the indemnity clause was overly complex and contained a critical flaw: a trigger dependent on confirmation by the "Highest Court of Appeal." This allowed the selling promoters, who were granted a Special Power of Attorney but lacked robust governance guardrails, to indefinitely defer liability by pursuing protracted appeals. Crucially, the deal failed to establish a targeted escrow or holdback for this known, quantified liability, a fundamental omission in M&A best practices.

The Supreme Court's judgment delivers vital lessons for M&A practitioners. It firmly interpreted the word "ensure" as an absolute, outcome-based obligation, not merely a best-efforts duty, thereby strengthening acquirers' positions. The court also clarified that a Consent Award functions as a contract with the finality of a tribunal's seal, aiding enforcement. Critically, it defined "crystallisation" of a liability, triggering an indemnity obligation, as occurring when an acquirer is compelled to make a deposit or payment (e.g., the ₹15.86 crore court-ordered deposit), rather than awaiting the exhaustion of all appeal processes.

This case underscores the paramount importance of meticulous M&A documentation. Dealmakers must incorporate targeted escrows or holdbacks for known liabilities, implement stringent litigation control provisions (including reporting requirements, settlement consent, and appeal restrictions for indemnifying parties), and draft unambiguous indemnity triggers linked to objective events like court orders or actual payments, independent of final appellate judgments. Adopting these measures can prevent protracted disputes and ensure the prompt and effective enforcement of commercial indemnity agreements.

title: Redefining the Auction Landscape: A Legal Analysis of India’s Mineral (Auction) Second Amendment Rules, 2026
description:
The Ministry of Mines promulgated India's Mineral (Auction) Second Amendment Rules, 2026, on March 30, 2026, significantly modifying the 2015 principal rules. These amendments introduce substantive and procedural reforms across Rules 8, 10, 18, 19G, 24, and a new Rule 25, aiming to modernize the mineral block auction regime.

A key provision (Rule 8(4)) exempts certain minor minerals (from Part D of the First Schedule, excluding Graphite, Phosphate, and Potash) from auction premium liability. This exemption applies if their aggregate estimated value constitutes less than ten percent of the block's total estimated resources, reducing the financial burden on bidders while strategically preserving premiums for critical minerals.

Transformative procedural reforms include the mandatory automation of Letter of Intent (LoI) issuance (Rules 10, 18, 19G). Upon receipt of prescribed payments and security, the LoI will be automatically generated via the Online Unified Mining Portal, curtailing administrative discretion and enhancing statutory certainty for preferred bidders.

The amendments also introduce flexibility for block exclusion and extended timelines. New Rule 10(2B) empowers State Governments to allow preferred bidders to exclude peripheral, infeasible portions of a block (up to 25% of resources). Additionally, execution timelines for Mining Lease Deeds (Rule 10(6)) are extended by two years, and Prospecting Licence Deeds (Rule 18(3)) by six months, under specific conditions, particularly for forest-encumbered blocks.

Bidder protections are enhanced through a new provision (Rule 10(6) Explanation) mandating the refund of security deposits and upfront payments within 90 days if an auction is annulled due to circumstances not attributable to the bidder. Furthermore, the second instalment of upfront payments is rescheduled to one year after LoI issuance, providing bidders with an extended liquidity window.

Perhaps the most structurally significant reform is the new Rule 25, which mandates the establishment of an Online Unified Mining Portal by the Central Government. From a specified date, all implementation and compliance activities under the 2015 rules will be exclusively conducted through this portal, signalling a comprehensive shift towards e-governance. Rule 24 has also been amended to expand the scope of composite licences within the auction framework.

Collectively, these amendments seek to reduce transactional friction, enhance investor confidence, and align India's mineral auction framework with contemporary standards of e-governance, streamlining processes and fostering a more predictable regulatory environment.

title: Karnataka Act No. 24 of 2026: The Legislative Armour Protecting Government School Lands
description:
The Karnataka Government Educational Institution’s Lands (Protection and Regularisation) Act, 2026, enacted on April 9, 2026, establishes a robust legal framework to secure land titles for Government Educational Institutions (GEIs). This significant legislation addresses historical ambiguities, undocumented titles, and informal arrangements, providing unprecedented legal certainty for public education infrastructure across Karnataka.

The Act's cornerstone, Section 3, dictates that any immovable property continuously occupied by a GEI for a minimum of 12 years shall be deemed vested in the State Government, free from all encumbrances. This statutory vesting overrides conventional property transfer laws, including the Transfer of Property Act and the Indian Succession Act, as well as the absence of registered instruments or donor death, effectively creating a specific form of statutory adverse possession for educational lands.

Section 4 implements a comprehensive bar on legal proceedings challenging rights or title in vested land where GEI occupation exceeds 12 years. Any pending suits concerning such lands abate upon the Act's commencement. A narrow exception exists for registered title holders: they may file a claim before the Grievance Redressal Authority within 30 days if they can demonstrate the land was never voluntarily dedicated to GEI use. The Authority may then recommend remedial action, such as land substitution.

Further reinforcing the State's position, Section 5 presumes public purpose dedication for land continuously used for education for over 12 years with community awareness. Section 6 codifies estoppel, preventing claims from those who knowingly acquiesced to GEI use without timely objection. The Act also prescribes a structured process for issuing Vesting Certificates, which serve as conclusive evidence of title, supported by designated Estate Officers responsible for land records and management.

A dedicated Grievance Redressal Authority (Revenue Court) handles claims within 60 days, with appellate recourse available at the Karnataka Appellate Tribunal. With its overriding effect, the Act signifies a decisive intervention, systematically regularizing GEI land holdings and imposing a critical, non-extendable 30-day limitation period for initial claims by affected parties.

title: Regulating the Price of Health: A Legal Analysis of India’s 2026 Drug Ceiling Price Notification
description:
On March 25, 2026, India's National Pharmaceutical Pricing Authority (NPPA) issued Statutory Order S.O. 1575(E), implementing revised ceiling prices for 767 scheduled pharmaceutical formulations, effective April 1, 2026. This action falls under the Drugs (Prices Control) Order, 2013 (DPCO, 2013), and incorporates an annual Wholesale Price Index (WPI) adjustment of 0.64956% for 2025 over 2024, reflecting minimal inflation in pharmaceutical input costs.

The updated schedule covers a wide range of essential medicines, including antibiotics, antiretrovirals, and oncological agents, with prices ranging from common formulations like Paracetamol (Rs. 0.93 per unit) to high-cost biologics such as Trastuzumab Injection (Rs. 56,039.24 per vial). These notified ceiling prices are exclusive of Goods and Services Tax (GST).

Manufacturers are mandated to immediately revise downward any Maximum Retail Price (MRP) exceeding the new ceilings. They have the discretion to revise MRPs upward if currently below the ceiling, within the WPI-linked limit. Manufacturers must submit updated price lists to the NPPA and State Drug Controllers via Form-V and report any upward revisions within fifteen days using Form-II. Retailers are required to conspicuously display these price lists. Non-compliance, including overcharging, leads to penalties under the DPCO, 2013, and the Essential Commodities Act, 1955.

This notification represents a routine but significant exercise of the NPPA's mandate to ensure affordability of essential medicines for the Indian population, with the low WPI adjustment ensuring price stability for the coming fiscal year.

title: MCX Circular: Information Sharing on Cybersecurity Incidents
description:
The Multi Commodity Exchange of India Limited (MCX), through Circular No. MCX/TECH/179/2026 dated April 6, 2026, has mandated all its trading members to report Cybersecurity Incidents for the quarter ending March 31, 2026. This directive underscores MCX's sustained commitment to strengthening cybersecurity frameworks, ensuring market integrity, and fortifying investor confidence within the commodity derivatives markets. It builds upon a series of prior regulatory communications on cybersecurity governance.

All trading members are obligated to submit these reports, including "nil reporting" even if no incidents occurred, to ensure MCX maintains a comprehensive and contemporaneous record of market participants' cybersecurity posture. Submissions must be made through MCX’s designated digital portal (https://member.mcxindia.com) by the deadline of April 15, 2026.

A significant procedural development, reiterated from a previous circular, introduces a technology-based cross-exchange submission mechanism. Trading members registered with both the National Stock Exchange of India Limited (NSE) and one or more other exchanges (BSE, MSE, MCX, NCDEX) are now required to submit their Cybersecurity Incident details exclusively to NSE. NSE will then transmit these reports to the respective exchanges, streamlining compliance by eliminating duplicate submissions for multi-exchange participants. Members not registered with NSE must continue making direct submissions to MCX. Non-compliance with these directives will render members liable for disciplinary action by MCX. Members are advised to ensure timely compliance through the appropriate channel before the April 15, 2026 deadline.

title: When The Fire Never Goes Out: Shankar V. State Of Rajasthan And The Conscience Of A Nation
description:
The Supreme Court of India recently upheld the conviction and life sentence of Shankar for the murder of his 20-year-old wife, Sugna Bai. She died four days after her husband set her on fire, her dying declaration, recorded by a magistrate, being the crucial evidence. It detailed her husband's actions, citing his alcoholism and suspicion. The Court rigorously applied the legal principle that a credible dying declaration alone can suffice for conviction, dismissing defense challenges and noting consistency with medical evidence.

Beyond its legal precision, the judgment features an extraordinary postscript—a judicial cry of anguish. The Court reviewed decades of progressive Indian laws and landmark judgments promoting women's rights and equality (e.g., Dowry Prohibition Act, Domestic Violence Act). Despite this extensive legal architecture and some urban progress, it highlighted a stark paradox: over 4.48 lakh crimes against women and 6,000 annual dowry-related deaths persist, particularly in rural and semi-urban areas. The Court noted that while laws have changed, the deep-seated patriarchal belief systems sustaining violence remain.

The Supreme Court stressed that law is necessary but insufficient for true social transformation; the failure is cultural, not legislative. It posed a profound question to the nation: "After decades of laws, schemes, reforms, and judicial recognition of equality... why does the control over women’s bodies, choices, and lives still persist so deeply within society?" Concluding that the answer lies with "We, the People of India," the Court underscored that while justice can be delivered, changing ingrained patriarchal mindsets demands the collective conscience and effort of all citizens, parents, and communities.

title: Can the Government Withdraw Tax Exemptions? Supreme Court of India Clarifies the Law
description:
The Supreme Court of India, in State of Maharashtra v. Reliance Industries Ltd. & Ors. (2026), addressed the State's power to withdraw tax exemptions. The case stemmed from Maharashtra's decision to withdraw electricity duty exemptions, initially granted to incentivize captive power generation, after industries had made substantial investments. The High Court had earlier struck down the withdrawal, prompting the State's appeal.

The Supreme Court clarified that tax exemptions are concessions, not vested or enforceable rights, and can be withdrawn unilaterally by the State in public interest. It asserted that doctrines like promissory estoppel and legitimate expectation, while applicable against the State, are not absolute bars against withdrawal when supervening public interest necessitates it. The Court also reiterated the principle of judicial restraint in matters of economic and fiscal policy, stating that interference is warranted only if decisions are arbitrary, irrational, or ultra vires.

Crucially, while affirming the State's power to modify or withdraw exemptions, the Court emphasized the need for fairness. It ruled that any such withdrawal must be prospective and accompanied by reasonable notice to allow affected industries to adjust. In this specific case, the Court determined that a one-year notice period was appropriate, thereby striking down the retrospective application of the withdrawal but upholding the State's power to modify the policy after this transition period.

This landmark judgment balances governmental policy autonomy with the legitimate expectations of investors. It serves as a significant precedent, ensuring that while the State can recalibrate fiscal policies in public interest, such changes are implemented with predictability, fairness, and a reasonable transition period, harmonizing economic governance with principles of equity and legal certainty for industries.

title: When Owners Become Trespassers: Reaffirming the Primacy of Possession in Criminal Law
description:
The Kerala High Court's recent judgment in Damodaran K. v. State of Kerala (2026:KER:26753) provides critical clarity on the intersection of criminal and property law, establishing that criminal trespass is an offense against possession, not ownership. This ruling highlights the importance of lawful means in resolving landlord-tenant disputes.

The case originated from an incident on May 11, 2009, where the revision petitioner, Damodaran K., a landlord, unlawfully entered premises he had rented to PW1, forcibly threw out household articles, and caused damage. Both the Judicial First Class Magistrate and the Additional Sessions Court convicted him under Indian Penal Code Sections 454 (house trespass) and 427 (mischief), imposing imprisonment and directing compensation.

On revision, the High Court, operating within its limited supervisory jurisdiction, affirmed the concurrent findings of guilt, relying on consistent eyewitness testimonies. Crucially, the Court asserted that even a true owner cannot unlawfully enter property in a tenant's lawful possession with criminal intent, thereby rejecting the notion that ownership absolves one of criminal liability for trespass.

However, recognizing the dispute's genesis in a landlord-tenant conflict rather than general criminal propensity, and considering the accused's lack of prior criminal antecedents, the High Court modified the sentence. It reduced the imprisonment for both offenses to "imprisonment till the rising of the Court," while upholding the ₹15,000 compensation to the victim. This judgment strongly reinforces the protection of tenants' possessory rights against landlords' self-help measures and underscores the imperative for property owners to pursue civil remedies rather than resorting to actions that incur criminal liability.

title: Fraud Vitiates All: Supreme Court’s Uncompromising Stand Against Insurance Fraud in United India Insurance v. Sayona Colors
description:
The Supreme Court of India's recent judgment in United India Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Sayona Colors Pvt. Ltd. unequivocally establishes that once fraud is proven in an insurance claim, no relief, partial or otherwise, can be granted. This landmark decision reinforces the fundamental principle that "fraud vitiates all" and underscores the paramount importance of maintaining the integrity of the insurance system.

The case involved Sayona Colors Pvt. Ltd., which claimed over twenty-eight crores after a fire incident, occurring shortly after a significant policy enhancement. The subsequent investigation by United India Insurance Co. Ltd. revealed overwhelming evidence of sophisticated fraud. Forensic analysis detected kerosene residues (a fire accelerant) at the origin of the blaze, conclusively negating the "short circuit" theory. Furthermore, documentary evidence exposed fabricated invoices and non-existent suppliers, all designed to artificially inflate the claim, while the claimant's conduct during the investigation was obstructive.

The National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (NCDRC) had erroneously granted partial compensation, fundamentally misinterpreting the legal principles governing fraudulent claims. The Supreme Court emphatically corrected this, reiterating that judgments or decrees obtained through fraud are nullities. The Court emphasized that once fraud is established, the entire edifice of the claim collapses, rendering any quantification of actual loss irrelevant, and leaving no room for partial or equitable relief.

Consequently, the NCDRC's order was set aside, the insurance claim stands repudiated, and the insurer was absolved of all liability. Significantly, the Supreme Court ordered a comprehensive criminal investigation by a Special Investigation Team, highlighting the severe repercussions of insurance fraud. This judgment sets a critical precedent, sending a strong deterrent message that fraudulent insurance claims will be met with total repudiation and potential criminal prosecution, reinforcing that the law cannot be an instrument for rewarding dishonesty.

title: From Periodic to Perpetual: Legal Implications of India’s New Food Safety Licensing Regime
description:
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has enacted significant amendments to its licensing and registration regulations, effective April 1, 2026, fundamentally reforming India’s food safety framework. The most pivotal change is the introduction of perpetual validity for FSSAI licenses and registrations, eliminating traditional periodic renewal requirements. This "pay-as-you-operate" model requires FBOs to remit annual fees to maintain indefinite validity, reducing administrative burden and providing enhanced operational certainty.

The reforms also establish revised turnover thresholds: FBOs with annual turnover up to ₹1.5 crore now require registration; those between ₹1.5 crore and ₹50 crore need a State License; and businesses exceeding ₹50 crore require a Central License. These adjustments particularly benefit small-scale operators, as street food vendors registered under the Street Vendors Act, 2014, receive deemed registration, and the expanded threshold shifts many small and medium enterprises to the less complex registration category. Instant registration provisions are also introduced for petty FBOs.

Furthermore, the new regime incorporates a risk-based inspection methodology, optimizing resource allocation by focusing regulatory scrutiny on high-risk operations while reducing unnecessary inspections for low-risk businesses. Transitional provisions ensure jurisdictional stability and continuity of license numbers, though FBOs migrating to lower categories due to revised thresholds cannot claim adjustments or refunds for previously paid fees. These amendments aim to create a more business-friendly regulatory environment by balancing administrative efficiency with the imperative of maintaining stringent food safety and public health standards.

title: Penalty Under the Employees Compensation Act Cannot be Passed on to Insurers
description:
The Supreme Court of India, in New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v. Rekha Chaudhary & Others (Feb 23, 2026), definitively ruled that an insurance company cannot be held liable for the statutory penalty imposed under Section 4A(3)(b) of the Employees’ Compensation Act, 1923. This penalty, incurred due to an employer's wilful delay in paying compensation, remains the employer's sole responsibility.

The case originated from a commercial driver's death, where the employer delayed compensation, leading to a penalty of Rs. 2,57,838/- by the Commissioner. While the insurer accepted liability for compensation and interest, the Delhi High Court erroneously transferred the penalty liability to the insurance company.

The Supreme Court meticulously analyzed Section 4A, noting that the 1995 amendment to the Act intentionally bifurcated the penalty clause from compensation and interest. This legislative change aimed to deter employers from delaying payments, as previously they could pass on penalties to insurers. The Court affirmed that contractual indemnity cannot override this statutory obligation arising from an employer's personal default, citing established precedents like Ved Prakash Garg v. Premi Devi.

Consequently, the Supreme Court set aside the High Court's decision on the penalty, directing the employer to pay it. This judgment reinforces employer accountability, ensures the Act's deterrent mechanism functions effectively, clarifies insurers' liabilities, and ultimately safeguards workers' interests by compelling prompt compensation payments.

title: Contractual Bar on Interest in Arbitration: Supreme Court Clarifies Limits on Arbitrator’s Powers
description:
In a significant ruling in Union of India v. Larsen & Toubro Ltd., the Supreme Court of India clarified the limitations on an arbitrator's power to award interest when a contract expressly prohibits it. The Court affirmed that where a contract explicitly bars the payment of interest, an arbitral tribunal cannot grant pre-award or pendente lite interest, even if framed as compensation. This underscores the paramountcy of party autonomy under Section 31(7)(a) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.

The dispute involved a contract between the Union of India and Larsen & Toubro for modernization works, which included General Conditions of Contract clauses (16(3) and 64(5)) explicitly prohibiting interest on amounts payable or on arbitral awards until the award date. While an arbitral tribunal awarded Larsen & Toubro a sum along with 12% post-award interest, and lower courts upheld this, the Supreme Court drew a crucial distinction.

The Supreme Court held that while the contractual prohibition governed pre-award and pendente lite interest, post-award interest operates under a distinct statutory framework (Section 31(7)(b)). As the contract only barred interest up to the date of the award, it did not explicitly exclude post-award interest. Consequently, the Court set aside the pre-award interest but upheld the grant of post-award interest, modifying the rate from 12% to 8% per annum.

This decision reinforces that arbitral tribunals must operate within the agreed contractual terms regarding pre-award interest, preventing circumvention through re-labeling. Simultaneously, it clarifies that post-award interest remains a statutory entitlement unless unambiguously excluded by the contract, balancing party autonomy with statutory safeguards. The ruling is particularly relevant for infrastructure and government contracts, where such interest-barring clauses are common.

title: Easing Compliance Burden: Analysis of Revised FSSAI Licensing Thresholds
description:
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has significantly recalibrated its regulatory landscape with an Order dated March 13, 2026, implementing revised turnover thresholds for food business categorization. This reform, stemming from recommendations by NITI Aayog's High-Level Committee, aims to reduce compliance burdens on food business operators, especially small and medium enterprises (SMEs), while preserving essential safety oversight. It amends the Food Safety and Standards (Licensing and Registration of Food Businesses) Regulations, 2011.

Key structural changes, formally notified on March 10, 2026, include addressing dual compliance for street vendors and introducing perpetual validity for FSSAI licenses/registrations, contingent on risk-based inspection outcomes rather than periodic renewal. Critically, the FSSAI is now empowered to flexibly specify and adjust turnover thresholds through delegated instruments, allowing for responsive updates.

Effective April 1, 2026, the new turnover thresholds are: Registration for annual turnover up to Rs. 1.5 crore; State License for turnover between Rs. 1.5 crore and Rs. 50 crore; and Central License for turnover exceeding Rs. 50 crore. These revised limits substantially raise previous static thresholds, significantly alleviating regulatory pressure on smaller enterprises.

This measured regulatory reform balances ease of doing business with public health protection. By substantially raising threshold limits and adopting risk-based oversight, it establishes a more proportionate, efficient, and adaptable food governance framework that benefits businesses, authorities, and consumers alike.

title: Transforming Food Business Regulation: An Analysis of the 2026 Amendments to FSS (Licensing and Registration) Regulations
description:
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has enacted the 2026 Amendment Regulations to its 2011 Licensing and Registration framework. These significant changes, developed after extensive consultations, aim to streamline regulations, bolster enforcement, and protect public health by introducing modifications across five key areas.

Firstly, the definition of "Petty Food Business Operator" is significantly expanded to formally include a broader informal sector. This encompasses street vendors, hawkers, small-scale industries, and food truck operators, ensuring appropriate regulatory oversight for a diverse range of informal food businesses.

Secondly, procedural innovations facilitate instant registration for low-risk categories, reducing bureaucratic delays, and grant FSSAI flexibility to tailor classification criteria based on business scale and risk. This integrates with the Street Vendors Act, 2014, deeming registered street vendors FSSAI-compliant if hygiene standards are met, thus eliminating dual registration burdens.

Thirdly, a dynamic license lifecycle establishes "deemed suspension" for non-payment of annual fees or non-submission of compliance returns, automatically prohibiting business operations. Mandatory closure procedures for food businesses are also introduced.

Finally, a new risk-based inspection and audit framework mandates periodical inspections and third-party audits. The frequency of these audits is determined by risk profiles and compliance history, optimizing regulatory resources by focusing oversight on high-risk establishments.

Collectively, these amendments signify a modernized, risk-proportional approach to food safety governance in India. They balance expanded regulatory reach to the informal sector with robust enforcement mechanisms, laying a strong foundation for future public health protection, contingent on effective implementation.

title: From Construction to Compliance: CG-RERA’s New Directive for Promoters Holding Completion Certificates
description:
CG-RERA Circular No. 146 (02 March 2026) issues binding directions under Section 37 of the RERA Act, 2016, mandating compliance for promoters of registered projects who obtained a Completion Certificate (CC) on or before 31 March 2024. This directive aims to ensure accurate, transparent, and updated post-completion project records, with online submissions active from 03 March 2026.

The circular establishes a two-tier compliance framework based on the project's registration and CC dates. For projects registered after obtaining the CC, promoters must submit only a copy of the CC, incurring no penal liability as completion predated RERA registration requirements. In contrast, projects registered before obtaining the CC face a more stringent obligation, requiring submission of the CC, Annexure-20, and a duly executed Affidavit cum Consent. These promoters are also liable for penalties under Sections 11 and 4(2)(l)(D) of the Act for potential post-registration non-compliance regarding promoter obligations and allottee consent.

The critical Affidavit cum Consent mandates declarations of project completion per approved plans, absence of deviations, and no further expansion. Importantly, clause 8 of the Affidavit requires promoters to accept any imposed penalty without contest. Satisfactory submissions lead to the project being classified as "Completed." Promoters are advised to identify their compliance category, compile necessary documents, and carefully review the affidavit's penalty waiver clause to avoid regulatory scrutiny and financial liability.

title: Air Canada v Commission: The Final Word on Extraterritoriality and Limitation Periods in EU Competition Law
description:
The CJEU, on February 26, 2026, dismissed Air Canada's appeal, significantly clarifying EU competition law on extraterritoriality, liability for single and continuous infringements, and the procedural standing of limitation periods. The case stemmed from a global airfreight cartel, with Air Canada challenging a re-adopted Commission decision imposing fines.

On extraterritorial jurisdiction, Air Canada argued for a substantive assessment of actual anticompetitive effects in the EEA for inbound routes, beyond the "qualified effects test." The CJEU affirmed the "implementation" and "qualified effects" tests as alternative bases for jurisdiction. It clarified that the Commission needs only prima facie evidence of foreseeable effects, shifting the burden to the undertaking for contrary proof. This reinforces the Commission's robust reach over non-EEA conduct affecting the internal market.

Crucially, regarding limitation periods under Article 25 of Regulation No. 1/2003, Air Canada contended it was a public policy matter requiring ex officio consideration by the General Court. The CJEU firmly rejected this. It held that Article 25 protects private interests, not public policy, and therefore must be expressly and timeously raised by the undertaking; otherwise, the defense is irrevocably lost. Disparities in outcomes depend solely on an undertaking's procedural diligence.

Air Canada also challenged its liability for routes it never directly operated within a single and continuous infringement. The CJEU dismissed this, reaffirming that liability requires contributing to the overarching anticompetitive plan and being aware of (or foreseeing) co-participants' conduct, not direct participation in every cartel element.

This judgment confirms the Commission's strong jurisdiction based on foreseeable effects, liability for unserved routes within a wider cartel, and critically, that limitation period defenses are not automatic and must be actively pursued by undertakings at the earliest opportunity.

title: Supreme Court Warns: Filing a Review Petition Does Not Excuse Non-Compliance with Court Orders
description:
The Supreme Court of India has issued a stern warning against the practice of using review petitions to excuse non-compliance with court orders, finding Chhattisgarh government officials in contempt. The ruling stemmed from the state's failure to create a supernumerary post as directed by May 2025, despite a clear three-month compliance window.

The Court found that compliance was deliberately withheld, being made conditional on a review petition. Officials admitted the ability to comply but chose not to, and critically, failed to seek any directions or clarifications from the Court regarding implementation difficulties. This conduct, the Court stated, constituted a "clear-cut case of contempt."

Reaffirming established legal principles, the judgment reiterated that contempt proceedings focus solely on whether an order was complied with, not its correctness or practicability. Furthermore, it underscored that anyone knowingly aiding or assisting in the violation of a court order, even if not an original party, can be held accountable, applying this to other government officials aware of the order.

The Court strongly deprecated the growing trend of governments using belated appeals or review petitions as a tactic to delay or avoid implementing court orders. It warned that such "brazen disobedience" threatens judicial authority and may border on criminal contempt, urging High Courts to deal with such conduct "with an iron hand," particularly when the State is involved.

In an extraordinary step, the judgment's copies will be circulated to top government officials nationwide, emphasizing that court orders are legal obligations, not optional policy suggestions. The contemnors were granted one final opportunity until March 24, 2026, to comply fully, failing which formal charges would be framed. This landmark ruling reinforces that the right to a review does not suspend the duty to comply, expands accountability, and signals a potential tightening of contempt jurisprudence against procedural delays.

title: Investment Sans Frontiers: Cabinet Refines PN3 FDI Regime With Beneficial Ownership Test And Fast-track Approvals
description:
The Union Cabinet has approved targeted amendments to the foreign direct investment (FDI) framework for investors from countries sharing land borders with India (LBCs), initially established by Press Note 3 of 2020 (PN3). The revised regime introduces a codified "Beneficial Owner" (BO) test, aligned with the Prevention of Money Laundering Rules, 2005, while retaining core national security safeguards.

A key feature is the establishment of a 10 percent non-controlling BO threshold, creating a safe harbor for non-strategic LBC-linked automatic route investments. These will not require prior government approval but mandate reporting by the Indian investee entity. Additionally, the framework now includes an expedited approval window for specified manufacturing sectors.

These amendments aim to broaden investment pathways, particularly for China-linked and China-exposed capital, by expanding market access and enhancing structuring options and execution certainty. India seeks to reinforce its appeal as a predictable and strategically resilient hub for long-term capital, balancing investment attraction with national security by ensuring ultimate control remains with resident Indian stakeholders.

title: When Two Become One: The Supreme Court on Group Insolvency in Real Estate
description:
In a landmark ruling on January 2, 2026, in Satinder Singh Bhasin v. Col. Gautam Mullick & Ors., the Supreme Court of India significantly clarified aspects of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, particularly concerning real estate project insolvencies involving interlinked entities. The case originated from the "Grand Venezia Commercial Tower" project, initiated by Bhasin Infotech and Infrastructure Private Limited, which later involved Grand Venezia Commercial Towers Private Limited for marketing, raising questions about their operational independence. The project faced severe delays, with assured returns ceasing in 2014 and legal possession never transferred due to unexecuted tripartite sublease deeds, a mandatory precondition.

The Court upheld the maintainability of a joint insolvency petition against both corporate debtors, citing compelling evidence of their operational and financial interdependence. This included common directors, shared communications, a joint venture, and significant inter-company transactions. The ruling affirmed that a single petition is warranted when corporate entities are intrinsically linked to the same project, ensuring value maximisation and preventing prejudice to allottees. This decision provides critical clarity for group insolvency jurisprudence, especially for financial creditors like homebuyers.

Furthermore, the judgment confirmed that the statutory threshold of one hundred allottees under Section 7(1) of the IBC must be assessed on the date of the petition's filing, a condition satisfied by the 103 allottees in this case. The Court also ruled that pre-registration amendments to the petitioner list, made after curing defects, are permissible under NCLT rules. It categorically rejected claims of project completion and legal possession, emphasizing that valid possession was contingent on the execution of tripartite sublease deeds, which were never fulfilled, a finding corroborated by multiple investigative reports.

This ruling marks a pivotal advancement in India's group insolvency framework, sanctioning collective accountability within complex corporate structures. It reinforces the IBC's philosophy of aligning legal remedies with economic realities, preventing corporate groups from exploiting structural complexities to evade obligations. The Court also implicitly called for Parliament to develop a comprehensive statutory framework for group insolvency resolution to complement this judge-made doctrine.

title: When Financial Rectitude Trumps Judicial Discipline: The IBC’s Uncompromising Supremacy Over Defunct Corporate Schemes
description:
The Supreme Court, in Omkara Assets Reconstruction Private Limited v Amit Chaturvedi & Ors (2026), delivered a pivotal judgment reaffirming the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code's (IBC) uncompromising supremacy over defunct corporate arrangements, prioritizing economic rehabilitation and protection of public funds over procedural technicalities. The case centered on a corporate debtor whose debt ballooned from Rs 10.6 crore to Rs 154 crore over two decades, attempting to shield itself from an IBC application using a 17-year-old, unimplemented Scheme of Arrangement (SOA) under the Companies Act, 1956.

The Court meticulously highlighted the fatal defects in the SOA. These included gross non-compliance with statutory timelines for scheme implementation and filing, the creditors' formal withdrawal of consent as early as 2009, and the scheme's economic redundancy, as it was based on 2008 debt figures but sanctioned in 2019 when the debt had almost doubled. Furthermore, post-2016 Companies Act amendments and transfer rules, the High Court lacked jurisdiction to sanction the scheme, as the proceeding should have been transferred to the NCLT.

Overturning the NCLAT's decision, which had stayed the IBC proceedings on grounds of "judicial discipline," the Supreme Court emphatically rejected this reasoning. It clarified that "judicial discipline" cannot be invoked by "tardy litigators" engaged in fractious litigation to jeopardize public funds or hold the economy hostage. The Court asserted the IBC's overriding effect under Section 238, stating it prevails over all other laws, including the Companies Act.

This landmark ruling underscores that the IBC is a comprehensive resolution statute, emphasizing revival and value maximization. It clarifies that a corporate debtor seeking compromise or arrangement must do so within the IBC framework, not by clinging to moribund schemes under the old Companies Act. The judgment sends a strong message that procedural delays and stale arrangements will not be permitted to undermine the IBC's objectives of swift, effective resolution and financial rectitude.

title: Biggest Number, Biggest Disappointment: The Supreme Court On Commercial Wisdom And The Myth Of The Best Offer
description:
The Supreme Court, in Torrent Power Ltd v Ashish Arjunkumar Rathi & Others, strongly affirmed the sanctity of the Committee of Creditors' (CoC) commercial wisdom under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC). The case involved the approved resolution plan for SKS Power, challenged by unsuccessful bidders (Torrent, Vantage, Jindal) who claimed the successful bidder, SEML, improperly improved its offer after the bid freeze through "clarifications."

The dispute arose when the CoC directed the Resolution Professional (RP) to seek clarifications from all bidders. SEML's responses, concerning the treatment of Bank Guarantees (BGs) and an upfront payment option of Rs 240 crore, were argued by the challengers as impermissible bid modifications. Both the NCLT and NCLAT rejected these claims, upholding SEML's plan.

The Supreme Court meticulously analyzed SEML's original plan. It determined that the BG clarification only addressed the mechanics and timing of securing banks during transition, not altering the total Rs 180.05 crore ultimately flowing to lenders. Similarly, the Rs 240 crore upfront option was always presented as the Net Present Value (NPV) of deferred payments; the clarification merely removed linguistic ambiguity about "discounted amount," not changing the offer's value. The Court established that a legitimate clarification explains what the plan already says, while a modification changes the offer's net present value.

Crucially, the Court reiterated the limited scope of judicial review in IBC matters, restricting appeals to specific statutory grounds and preventing courts from substituting their judgment for the CoC's commercial decisions. It condemned the "strategic use of the judicial system by unsuccessful resolution applicants" to obstruct the process, emphasizing that the IBC does not mandate selecting the "highest bidder" but considers overall feasibility and viability. Such litigation undermines the IBC's goals of speed, value maximization, and finality, causing economic harm and breeding strategic obstruction.

This landmark judgment clearly differentiates legitimate clarifications from impermissible modifications, firmly rejects attempts to disguise commercial complaints as procedural violations, and unequivocally underscores that the IBC's design demands respect for creditor authority and timely, unhindered implementation of resolution plans.

title: Bureau of Indian Standards (Conformity Assessment) Amendment Regulations, 2026: A Paradigm Shift in Quality Assurance and Regulatory Compliance
description:
The Bureau of Indian Standards (Conformity Assessment) Amendment Regulations, 2026, enacted on February 25, 2026, significantly overhaul the 2018 framework, modernizing India’s quality assurance landscape. These amendments, under Sections 12, 13, and 39 of the BIS Act, 2016, introduce substantial modifications to the processes governing the grant, continuation, renewal, suspension, and cancellation of licences and certificates of conformity.

A key change is the mandatory annual advance fee payment for licence grant, continuation, and renewal (Regulations 5, 6, 13, 14). Failure to comply triggers a graduated response outlined in Regulation 8: non-payment suspends a licence for 90 days. During this period, paying the annual fee plus a ₹5000 late fee revokes the suspension. However, if the fee remains unpaid, the licence is cancelled. This mechanism applies similarly to certificates (Regulation 16). Regulations 10, 11, 18, and 19 mandate a 21-day notice before suspension or cancellation, except for non-payment cases where specific procedures under Regulation 8/16 apply.

Schedule-II introduces a new taxonomy of ten conformity assessment schemes, categorized by application (products, systems, services, processes) and on-site assessment requirements. Notably, Scheme-II is completely revised for registration based on self-declaration of conformity (Type C assessment). This allows manufacturers to obtain a licence using test reports from third-party laboratories, supported by self-declarations and affidavits. The scheme involves a detailed application process, including nominating an Indian representative for foreign manufacturers.

Scheme-II also features an accessible fee structure: ₹1000 for application, annual licence, and renewal, plus a ₹25000 annual processing fee. Significant concessions are provided for Micro (80%), Small (50%), and Medium (20%) Enterprises until May 2029, and a 20% concession for all MSMEs thereafter. Licences and certificates under most schemes have an initial validity of up to five years, renewable for similar periods, with annual advance fee payments. Comprehensive surveillance mechanisms, including market sampling and complaint investigation, are incorporated.

These amendments foster greater financial discipline, enhance procedural clarity, and expand certification access, especially through the self-declaration scheme. The balanced enforcement mechanism and MSME concessions support inclusive industrial policy. Stakeholders must align compliance procedures with these new requirements, ensuring market access for quality products and consumer protection in India.

title: Constitutional Protection of Live-in Relationships: Delhi High Court Affirms Adult Autonomy in Kartik v. State of NCT of Delhi
description:
In a landmark judgment on February 24, 2026, the Delhi High Court, in Kartik & Anr. v. State of NCT of Delhi & Ors., definitively affirmed the constitutional rights of consenting adults in live-in relationships and mandated State protection against familial interference. This ruling, reinforcing individual autonomy, highlights the State's positive obligation to safeguard fundamental rights under the Constitution.

The case involved Kartik and his partner, both consenting adults, who chose to cohabit, formalizing their commitment through a Live-in Relationship Agreement. They faced severe threats and harassment from the partner's father (Respondent No. 4). Seeking relief under Article 226 of the Constitution, the petitioners argued that their fundamental right to life and personal liberty (Article 21) was gravely jeopardized by these threats.

The Court, referencing Supreme Court precedents like Nandakumar v. State of Kerala and Shafin Jahan v. Asokan K.M., established that live-in relationships, when entered into by consenting adults, are "in a way akin to marriage" and deserve constitutional protection. It emphasized that societal morals and prejudices cannot override constitutional guarantees. The judgment underscored that Articles 19 (freedom of movement and residence) and 21 (right to life and personal liberty) impose a positive duty on the State to protect individuals from threats, including those emanating from private parties like family members.

Crucially, the Court unequivocally stated that no person, including parents, has the right or authority to interfere with the personal choices of adult children regarding their relationships. Threats to life and liberty from family members are impermissible, and the State is constitutionally obligated to intervene and provide protection. The petition was allowed, directing immediate police protection for the couple and ensuring continued assistance even if they relocate. This decision significantly contributes to a more inclusive understanding of personal relationships in Indian law, affirming adult autonomy and the right to dignity against societal prejudice and familial coercion.

title: Matrimonial Cruelty as a Continuing Offence: Kerala High Court on Delay, Evidence and Section 498A IPC
description:
The Kerala High Court, in Pravin Kumar Vs. State of Kerala, provided significant clarifications on the scope and application of Section 498A of the Indian Penal Code, upholding a conviction related to dowry-related cruelty. The judgment articulated three key principles: the restricted ambit of revisional powers, the characterization of matrimonial cruelty as a continuing offence, and the necessity of a contextual approach to delay and testimonial inconsistencies in such prosecutions.

Section 498A IPC, enacted to protect married women from cruelty by their husbands or relatives, targets dowry-related violence and domestic abuse. The Court reiterated that revisional jurisdiction under Sections 397 and 401 CrPC is limited, not appellate. Interference is warranted only if findings are perverse, illegal, or manifestly unreasonable, precluding a mere re-appreciation of evidence.

Crucially, the High Court affirmed matrimonial cruelty as a "continuing offence," recognizing it as a sustained pattern of harassment rather than isolated incidents. Consequently, delay in lodging a complaint does not, by itself, undermine the prosecution's credibility. The judgment acknowledged socio-legal realities like hope for reconciliation, family pressure, social stigma, economic dependence, and fear that often cause delayed reporting, emphasizing a contextual rather than mechanical assessment of such delays.

The Court also stressed that victim testimony in Section 498A cases must be evaluated with sensitivity, cautioning against a hyper-technical approach that would defeat the provision's purpose. Minor discrepancies from rustic witnesses do not erode the core prosecution case when the overall testimony inspires confidence. In this instance, the conviction was supported by the victim's consistent testimony, medical evidence of injuries, and corroboration from her parents. While confirming the conviction, the High Court exercised discretion to reduce the substantive sentence, maintaining a calibrated approach.

This decision significantly advances the interpretative framework of Section 498A IPC by reinforcing the narrow limits of revisional jurisdiction, establishing cruelty as a continuing offence, and mandating a sensitive, contextual evaluation of delay and evidence. It effectively balances procedural discipline with the substantive goal of safeguarding married women from dowry-related abuse.

title: The Limits of Criminal Law in Debt Recovery: Examining the One Time Settlement Conundrum Through the Lens of Section 482 Cr.P.C.
description:
The Delhi High Court, in Mahender Singh v. State (Govt of NCT of Delhi), provided significant clarity on quashing criminal proceedings under Section 482 Cr.P.C. in cases stemming from failed banking settlements. The dispute involved Central Bank of India and borrower companies whose overdrafts became Non-Performing Assets. A One Time Settlement (OTS) for Rs. 6 crores was proposed, with the borrower, Suresh Goel, depositing Rs. 58 lakhs in a 'No-Lien' account.

The OTS failed as Goel couldn't make the balance payment by the deadline, leading to its automatic cancellation and the revival of the original Rs. 13 crore liability. Subsequently, the Bank assigned the debt to UV Asset Reconstruction Company Limited. Goel then filed a criminal complaint, alleging criminal breach of trust for the non-refund of Rs. 58 lakhs, cheating through an allegedly antedated assignment agreement, and conspiracy. The Metropolitan Magistrate proceeded to summon bank officials.

The High Court quashed the complaint and summoning order, emphasizing that the dispute was fundamentally civil. It ruled that the Rs. 58 lakhs, upon OTS failure, was legitimately adjustable by the Bank as per the agreed contractual terms, negating any criminal breach of trust. The Court accepted that the assignment agreement's effective date preceded the purchase of stamp paper and registration, terming it a procedural formality, not evidence of a pre-existing conspiracy to cheat. The judgment reiterated that criminal law cannot be employed as a tool for debt recovery, underscoring the absence of dishonest intention at the inception of the transaction, which is essential for cheating, and the lack of entrustment for criminal breach of trust. This decision reinforces that commercial disputes, particularly those arising from contractual defaults, must be resolved through civil remedies, protecting financial institutions from the misuse of the criminal process.

title: Defining Safety in Food Contact Materials: Analysis of the Draft Food Safety and Standards (Packaging) Amendment Regulations, 2026
description:
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has published the draft Food Safety and Standards (Packaging) Amendment Regulations, 2026, proposing significant revisions to India’s food packaging safety framework. This initiative, mandated by the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, aims to align India's standards with international benchmarks, addressing technological advancements and emerging risks in food contact materials.

The proposed amendments introduce five crucial new definitions to expand and clarify the regulatory scope: 1. Food Contact Material: Encompasses any material, including active and intelligent packaging, intended to come into contact with food, establishing the fundamental requirement that it must not adversely affect food or human health. 2. Food Grade Contact Material: Defines materials used for food handling and packaging, mandating compliance with specific safety standards to prevent endangerment of health or unacceptable changes to food characteristics. 3. Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP): Provides a regulatory definition for the enclosure of food in packages where the internal atmosphere is intentionally altered to extend shelf life and maintain food quality. 4. Non-Intentionally Added Substances (NIAS): Recognizes chemicals present in food contact materials that were not deliberately added (e.g., impurities, degradation products), acknowledging their potential health risks and the need for their regulation. 5. Aseptic Packaging: Defines the process of filling a sterilized product into a sterilized container within a sterile environment to prevent contamination, crucial for ensuring the safety of long-shelf-life liquid foods.

These comprehensive amendments are designed to enhance consumer protection, improve enforcement capabilities, and establish clearer safety benchmarks for the evolving food packaging industry. The draft regulations are currently undergoing a 60-day public consultation period, allowing stakeholders to provide feedback before finalization and notification, marking a critical step in modernizing India's food safety governance.

title: Ensuring Integrity in Food Testing: FSSAI’s Enforcement of Statutory Obligations through InFoLNet Integration and NABL Accreditation Compliance
description:
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has issued a critical order to address widespread non-compliance among its notified food testing laboratories, aiming to strengthen the national food safety regime. This intervention, under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, and the 2018 Recognition and Notification of Laboratories Regulations, mandates rigorous adherence to statutory obligations. The non-compliance with Regulation 9 of the 2018 Regulations posed significant risks to food safety integrity and public confidence.

The order establishes four key compliance pillars. First, laboratories must promptly upload all test reports to the Information and Food Laboratory Network (InFoLNet) portal, ensuring real-time transparency and data accessibility for regulatory authorities. Second, they must maintain accurate and updated profiles on InFoLNet, covering accreditation validity, contact details, participation in proficiency testing, manpower details, and analytical capacity declarations.

Third, laboratories are directed to strictly follow FSSAI-validated and prescribed test methods and operate exclusively within the scope of their National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories (NABL) accreditation. This ensures standardized, scientifically rigorous, and internationally benchmarked quality. Fourth, all test reports must conform to prescribed formats without deviation, facilitating data comparison, aggregation, and legal admissibility.

FSSAI has explicitly warned that continued non-compliance will lead to appropriate enforcement actions, including suspension or cancellation of laboratory recognition/notification and imposition of penalties. This decisive move underscores FSSAI’s commitment to upholding scientific integrity, protecting public health, and ensuring reliable, transparent, and accountable food testing across India.

title: Transferee Pendente Lite and the Bar Under Order XXI Rule 102: Supreme Court’s Affirmation of Lis Pendens in Money Decree Execution Proceedings
description:
The Supreme Court, in R. Savithri Naidu, clarified lis pendens application to money decrees and the bar under Order XXI Rule 102 CPC on transferees pendente lite. This thwarts judgment debtors from defeating execution via property alienation, ensuring procedural safeguards provide actual relief to decree-holders.

The case involved a 2001 arbitral award for money recovery obtained by Cotton Corporation of India (CCI) against Lakshmi Ganesh Textiles. After its challenge was dismissed in 2013, the property was transferred in 2015 to R. Savithri Naidu (Appellant), related to the debtor. When CCI began execution in 2019, Appellant claimed bona fide purchase. Lower courts dismissed her claim, deeming her a barred pendente lite transferee due to circumstances including non-production of a key tripartite agreement.

The Supreme Court upheld these decisions. It found Appellant lacked bona fides, citing her familial link and failure to produce the tripartite agreement, implying notice. The Court reiterated that lis pendens (Section 52 TPA) applies to money suits, with pendency continuing until complete decree satisfaction, as affirmed in Danesh Singh.

For Order XXI Rule 102, a pendente lite transferee acquires property after original suit institution. The arbitration began in 1999, award (deemed decree) in 2001; thus, the 2015 transfer made Appellant a barred transferee. This prevents debtors from frustrating decrees via post-decree alienations.

Dismissing the appeal, the Supreme Court strengthens the execution framework. It deters collusive transfers, ensuring litigants realize their reliefs, upholding judicial process integrity.

title: Finality of Section 11 Determinations under the Pre-2015 Arbitration Regime: Supreme Court Reaffirms Res Judicata Principles
description:
In a significant ruling, the Supreme Court of India, in M/s Eminent Colonizers Private Limited v. Rajasthan Housing Board, affirmed the binding and conclusive nature of judicial determinations made under Section 11 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, specifically for proceedings initiated prior to the 2015 amendment. The judgment clarifies that once the existence and validity of an arbitration agreement are implicitly or expressly determined at the Section 11 stage, this issue cannot be reopened in subsequent Section 34 proceedings, due to the doctrine of res judicata.

The dispute arose from construction contracts where M/s Eminent Colonizers Private Limited (Appellant) sought the appointment of an arbitrator under Section 11 of the pre-2015 Act. The High Court appointed a sole arbitrator, an order which the Respondent, Rajasthan Housing Board, did not challenge. Following arbitral awards in favor of the Appellant, the Respondent filed Section 34 applications, primarily contending that the relevant contractual clause did not constitute a valid arbitration agreement. The Commercial Court and High Court concurred with the Respondent, setting aside the awards, leading to the appeal before the Supreme Court.

The Supreme Court emphasized that arbitral proceedings were governed by the pre-2015 legal position, adhering to the principles laid down in SBP & Co. v. Patel Engineering Ltd. This precedent established that Section 11 powers are judicial, requiring a conclusive determination of a valid arbitration agreement, which attains finality under Section 11(7). The Court reasoned that the High Court's act of appointing an arbitrator implicitly confirmed the existence of a valid agreement, and the Respondent's failure to challenge this order made it final.

Crucially, the Court clarified the distinction between precedent (operating in rem) and res judicata (operating in personam). It held that even if the Section 11 order lacked detailed reasoning, it operated as res judicata between the specific parties, precluding re-examination of the same issue. This position differs from the post-2015 regime under Section 11(6A), where the court’s inquiry is confined to a prima facie examination, leaving deeper issues to the arbitral tribunal.

Allowing the appeals, the Supreme Court ruled that the lower courts erred in re-examining the arbitration agreement's validity. The issue was concluded by res judicata, making the setting aside of awards on that ground unsustainable. The matter was remitted for consideration of other Section 34 objections, excluding the now-finalized issue of the arbitration agreement's existence and validity. This judgment reinforces procedural finality, judicial coherence, and the integrity of arbitration under the pre-2015 regime, highlighting the necessity of promptly challenging Section 11 orders.

title: “Khamosh” No More: Actor-Politician Shatrughan Sinha Moves Court To Stop Misuse Of His Image, Voice, And Catchphrase
description:
Actor-politician Shatrughan Sinha has petitioned the Bombay High Court to safeguard his personality and publicity rights from unauthorized commercial exploitation. He seeks protection for distinct attributes like his name, voice, signature phrase "Khamosh," image, likeness, caricature, and signature, asserting their commercial value and strong association with his identity over decades.

Sinha's plea details the extensive misuse of his persona, including the creation of AI-generated images designed to ridicule him, memes, GIFs, fake social media accounts, and the unauthorized sale of merchandise such as posters and t-shirts. These activities not only deceive the public into believing his endorsement but also inflict reputational damage.

Initially, the High Court questioned the maintainability of a commercial IP action based on personality rights. In response, Sinha's legal team argued that these rights are independently enforceable under common law principles like "passing off," fundamental rights (Articles 19(1)(a) and 21, encompassing the right to privacy), and Section 38B of the Copyright Act, 1957, which protects performers' moral rights against distortion. Satisfied with these submissions, the Court has reserved the matter for its orders.

title: Enforcement of Retiral Benefits and Salary Arrears in Contempt Jurisdiction
description:
The Allahabad High Court, in Contempt Application (Civil) No. 2193 of 2025, unequivocally affirmed that timely disbursement of retiral dues and salary arrears is a legally enforceable right, not subject to administrative discretion. This decision arose from the U.P. State Employees Welfare Corporation's failure to comply with earlier judicial directives, despite the applicant's entitlement being upheld after the Corporation's appeal was dismissed.

The Court scrutinized a personal affidavit filed by the Corporation's Executive Director, finding it inaccurate and incomplete, specifically regarding outstanding salary arrears. Furthermore, the Court identified impermissible deductions totaling ₹4,55,100.38 from the applicant's retiral dues, which included amounts for "Prostsahan Agrim," "Tyohar Agrim," and GST recovery. The Court ruled that these deductions lacked statutory authority, emphasizing that retiral benefits are vested entitlements that cannot be reduced or withheld arbitrarily. Concerns were also raised about the non-disbursement of state-provided financial assistance intended for these payments.

In response, the Court granted the Executive Director a final one-month period to clear all retiral dues without deductions and fully pay the salary arrears, warning of contempt charges for non-compliance. Additionally, the Principal Secretary, Food and Civil Supplies, Uttar Pradesh, was directed to investigate the reasons for the continued non-payment despite judicial orders and report on actions taken against defaulting officers. This judgment underscores the critical importance of individual and institutional accountability in public administration, reinforcing that public authorities are legally bound to implement judicial directions promptly and in strict accordance with the law.
